topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien in my hands. It was my essay, the one I poured my soul into—a meandering exploration of a summer spent restoring an antique clock. But the font was wrong. The header wasn't mine; it was a generic institutional blue. Beneath the title, in small, almost apologetic print, was a footnote: "Example Submission: Successful Narrative Structure." My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles jump beneath my skin. I crumpled the paper, the sound sharp in the quiet library corner. Every nerve ending felt prickly, a tight wire humming with intense frustration. I stared at the screen where the published version sat, the polished prose mocking my own raw, messy draft. I pushed my chair back with a screech that made a nearby student jump. I needed to rip this thing apart, to destroy the evidence that someone else had stolen the shape of my voice.

[story 2]

I traced the perfectly formed paragraph with a fingertip, the ink cool against my skin. It was flawless. Too flawless. I reread the opening sentence, the cadence so smooth, so effortlessly engaging. A hot flush crept up my neck, spreading across my cheeks. I snatched the printed piece, my fingers trembling slightly. "This isn't mine," I muttered, the words sounding thin and brittle in the echoing hallway. The injustice of it churned in my gut, a heavy, acidic pressure demanding release. I paced three steps, then stopped abruptly, planting my feet firmly. I looked around the empty corridor, feeling a sudden, intense need to shout, to make some noise that would shatter the silence and expose the deception. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I felt a deep, internal churning, a furious energy building behind my sternum that threatened to boil over.

[story 3]

When I finally found the file, the realization hit me like a physical blow. My carefully crafted narrative, the one that felt uniquely *me*, was sitting there, neatly packaged as a template. My hands clenched into fists so tight my knuckles shone white. I let out a low, involuntary sound—a sharp expulsion of air that tasted metallic. I stood up, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. I walked toward the window, the city lights blurring through the glass, reflecting the agitated state of my face. The air felt too thin to breathe properly. I turned back to the screen, my gaze fixed, burning with a cold, sharp intensity. I wanted to scream at the invisible committee, to demand accountability, but the sound caught in my throat, replaced by a relentless, vibrating tension that made my entire body feel coiled and ready to snap.
